In this lesson, we're going to talk about maximization offer number one, the Reverse Add-on. Looking at the map, you can see that this is the first add-on offer that comes immediately after the customer captivation offer. Immediately after a prospect places their order for the customer captivation offer, they are brought to the Reverse Add-on Offer page. Let's talk about some of the elements of the Reverse Add-on Offer. Like with all add-on offers, this offer needs to be congruent. It needs to be congruent with the customer captivation offer. This can't be just a random upsell or random add-on offer. It needs to be tied, linked with whatever it is that you are offering the prospects in the customer captivation offer. Whatever topic, whatever area, whatever element it is that you're covering in there, the add-on offer needs to be congruent. What you offer these new customers needs to add value to the customer captivation offer. It should either improve results that they get with the customer captivation offer or it should provide them with the next step after the customer captivation offer. The key is that your Reverse Add-on Offer in no way, shape or form implies that the customer captivation offer is incomplete. In other words, one of the biggest mistakes that you can make with any kind of upsell, but especially the Reverse Add-on, is to imply that, well, this is really what you need or this gives you the complete story or this is everything that you were after. No, all you'll do is piss off new customers. It needs to be congruent with the initial offer. It needs to add value to the initial offer and it can no way imply that that initial offer is incomplete or will not deliver on what was promised. The reason why this add-on offer is huge and why this thing converts at a rate higher than any other type of upsell that I've ever seen as the first upseller, first upgrade offer after an initial customer captivation offer is because it leverages typical consumer add-on psychology. The Reverse Add-on Offer is an offer that is priced up to 80% of the customer captivation offer. In other words, it's at a lower price point than the original customer captivation offer. It's not at a price point higher and it's certainly not at a price point three times higher. Remember, keep in mind, think about the way it works in the real world, the real world of commerce. Any time a consumer is presented with the opportunity to upgrade their order or to add something on to their order, it's never more than what the original purchased item was priced at. It's always a fraction of what the original item was priced at. If somebody goes through the drive-through at McDonald's and they're asked if they want a biggie size that are up-sized that are super-sized at whatever it's called, the up-sizing it is always a lower price point than the main meal that they ordered. If a man were to go in and purchase a new suit and spend $2,000 on a new suit, when he's then offered the opportunity to add a tie to it for $80 or a beautiful shirt for $150 or a pair of shoes for $200, those things are always a fraction of the two-piece order. They're never offered something that is multiples of the initial offer. This type of offer becomes brain-dead simple. It becomes a no-brainer for your new customers to take advantage of as long as it's congruent and it adds value and it's something that they want. You're tapping into what it is that they desire most. Again, it is priced up to 80%. It's 80% of the price of the customer captivation offer. The reverse add-on is presented as a video sales letter and it does not have a delayed add to cart button. It's presented as a VSL or a short-form sales letter and the add to cart button is immediately displayed. Compared to the customer captivation offer, the customer captivation offer has a delayed add to cart button. It pops up when the offer is presented and I already explained the psychology behind why we do that. In this case, because of the way it's priced, because it's a reversed add-on, it becomes much more of an impulse purchase. It becomes much easier for the new customer to make a decision. We have the add to cart button displayed immediately. Let me give you a simple example of a reverse add-on. This was an offer that you were presented with when you became a six-figure funnel formula client. The initial launch offer price for the six-figure funnel formula is $297. The very first add-on offer was priced at $199. That's 67% of the initial offer. It's also congruent with the six-figure funnel formula because it's all about next-level marketing funnel maps. It in no way takes away from anything that the six-figure funnel formula promised or delivers. It adds value because it gives clients the ability to learn new marketing funnels once they reach a higher level. This is a great example of a reverse add-on offer at work.
